planting.note: Plant fresh seeds or container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; camphor tree is an extremely vigorous, large evergreen tree that can reach 50–100 feet. Consider size carefully before planting — it becomes a major landscape tree. It is invasive in Florida.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; camphor tree is a majestic, aromatic evergreen tree from China and Japan, the source of natural camphor extracted from the wood and leaves. Every part of the tree contains camphor and 1,8-cineole, giving the foliage a powerful medicinal scent when crushed. It is an important timber, medicinal, and aromatic tree in traditional Asian culture. In the American South (particularly Texas and California), it is a common large shade tree planted for its ornamental value and fragrant foliage. NOTE: Camphor tree is classified as invasive in Florida and should not be planted there; it is suitable for other Gulf Coast states. The berries are consumed by birds but the plant as a whole is toxic to humans. Provides exceptional shade and is remarkably drought tolerant once established.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock after last frost in fertile, well-drained, sandy loam, slightly acidic soil in full sun; true cinnamon is frost-tender and suitable for zones 10–12 outdoors. In zone 9, grow in a large container and bring indoors in winter.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, sandy loam, slightly acidic soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; true cinnamon (Ceylon cinnamon) from Sri Lanka is considered the 'real' cinnamon of culinary tradition — lighter, sweeter, and more delicate than the cassia cinnamon (Cinnamomum cassia) sold in most North American supermarkets. True cinnamon has very low coumarin content compared to cassia, making it safer for regular consumption. The cinnamon spice is harvested from the inner bark of shoots cut from coppiced trees — the thin bark curls into the familiar quills as it dries. In the home garden, the tree provides an ornamental, fragrant, tropical presence; the young leaves can be used as a bay leaf substitute. In zones 9 and below, grow in a large container and harvest leaves and small stems for culinary use. Coppice regularly to keep the plant at a manageable size.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; banana shrub is a classic Deep South garden plant, reliably perennial in zones 7–10 and producing its delightfully banana-scented flowers in spring and summer.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; banana shrub is a classic evergreen shrub of the South, related to magnolias and producing small, creamy-yellow flowers edged in maroon-purple that emit an intense fragrance reminiscent of ripe bananas — particularly potent in warm, humid air. The fragrance carries considerable distance and can be detected from many feet away when the plant is in full bloom in spring and early summer. The evergreen foliage is attractive year-round. This plant has been grown in Southern gardens for well over a century and is among the most beloved fragrant shrubs for the warm-climate garden. In zone 9a it is a reliable, low-maintenance garden anchor that thrives with minimal care once established. A few individual flowers in a small vase can fill a room with fragrance.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors or plant container stock after last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; champaca is frost-tender and suitable for zones 10–12 outdoors. In zone 9 it may survive in warm, sheltered microclimates or be grown as a large container specimen.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; champaca is a large tropical tree from the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asia that produces some of the most intensely fragrant flowers in the botanical world — the golden-orange, magnolia-like blooms have a complex, fruity-floral-spicy scent used in 'Joy' by Jean Patou (once called the world's most expensive perfume) and numerous other fine fragrances. The flowers are sacred in Hindu and Buddhist traditions and used extensively as temple offerings across South and Southeast Asia. In India, garlands of champaca flowers are worn in the hair. The essential oil, extracted by enfleurage or solvent extraction, is extraordinarily expensive. In the home garden in zone 9, grow as a large container specimen moved indoors in winter, where even a few flowers will perfume an entire room.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Fortune's osmanthus is a hybrid between O. fragrans and O. heterophyllus, combining the exceptional fragrance of the first with the cold hardiness of the second.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Fortune's osmanthus is a hybrid evergreen shrub combining the most desirable traits of its two parents — the extraordinary apricot-peach fragrance of the tea olive (O. fragrans) with the improved cold hardiness and dense, attractive, spiny-leaved foliage of the holly osmanthus (O. heterophyllus). The tiny, cream-white flowers in fall produce the same intensely sweet, far-carrying fragrance as tea olive, perfuming the garden from a considerable distance. This hybrid is cold-hardy to about 0°F (USDA zone 7) compared to tea olive's zone 8 hardiness, making it the better choice for the colder end of the zone 7–9 range. An excellent, deer-resistant, relatively low-maintenance evergreen shrub for the partial-shade garden in zones 7–9.
